plan.description:
The study deals with the mussel, Mytilus galloprovincialis specifically to clone and functionally characterise the endocrine regulatory factors (i.e. ligand and receptor pairs) involved in the control of reproduction.
This project will generate sequence data of ligands and G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs) - first identified via bioinformatic methods and second, confirmed via molecular biology and cell receptor binding assays. This includes investigating the spatial distribution of the ligands and receptors via Quantitative PCR and assess tissue-specific roles.
Additionally, we will validate potential molecular sex markers for this species of mussel using PCR and histology.
The purpose of the DMP is to safe-guard the results as they are created, and to make it publicly available to the research community and sponsors.
The genome of this species is already published and partially annotated. We have used this genome for bioinformatic mining as a starting point to identify and to verify by cloning and sequencing the ligands/receptors of interest. All the molecular sequences that we will produce and verify will become available in Open Access publications.
plan.title: PID 38757 Heather G. Marco : Hormonal control of reproduction in mussels: fundamental knowledge for aquaculture

blueprint.label: AQUASERV DMP Blueprint

systemField.label: Title of Plan
systemField.description: Please provide a name for your DMP Plan

systemField.label: Description
systemField.description: Briefly describe the context and purpose of the DMP

systemField.label: Language
systemField.description: Select the language of your DMP

referenceField.label: Researchers
referenceField.description: Add here the names of people that have produced, processed, analysed the data described in the DMP.

referenceField.label: Organizations
referenceField.description: Add here the names of the organizations contributing to the creation and revision of the DMPs

extraField.label: Introduction
extraField.description: Ethical considerations in science become crucial whenever your research involves potential harm to living organisms, ecosystems, or communities. If your research fits into these criteria please answer following questions. As a default please fill the Horizon Europe - Ethics template, however other templates are available.

extraField.label: Introduction
extraField.description: Please select a template to describe your dataset that best fits your research topic. You can chose more than one template. All the templates prepared for the AQUASERV project are called "AQUASERV | category | datatype". If you can't find a template that fits your research, please contact aquaservhelpdesk@ualg.pt

blueprint.description: This is the main AQUASERV blueprint containing common questions about you and your project, as well as multiple "templates." The common questions need to be answered only once. Use one "template" for each dataset you are generating. For example, if you generate genomics data, use the genomics template (already available); if you generate LC-MS data, use the LC-MS template (coming soon). If you generate more than one type of dataset, you can combine multiple templates into one DMP "plan."

blueprintValue.value: Aquaserve project on mussel neuropeptides and their GPCRs. Molecular datasets of sequences, spatial distribution via quantitative PCRs, and the deorphanisation of identified GPCRs potentially of the GnRH receptor Superfamily.

reference.label: CENTRO DE CIENCIAS DO MAR DO ALGARVE

description.description: Hormones regulate reproduction and many other processes in animals, including bivalves, like mussels. We investigate potential hormones that may influence the physiology of the edible mussel. We investigate the GnRH receptor Superfamily and the family of peptides that are known from other invertebrates to influence reproduction.
description.label: AQUASERV PID 38757 Heather G. Marco/hormonal systems of the Mediterranean mussel

descriptionTemplate.label: AQUASERV template
descriptionTemplate.description: A template of Horizon Europe adopted and simplified for AQUASERV

fieldSet.title: Will you re-use any existing data and what will you re-use it for? State the reasons if re-use of any existing data has been considered but discarded.
fieldSet.description:
example: Re-use existing data

I will look for data (occurrences of a range of species) from EMODnet to complement my study of biodiversity of the Baltic Sea in 2025

fieldSet.title: What types and formats of data will the project generate or re-use?
fieldSet.description:
example: 
Type of data
    I will be collecting images (jpg or png, probably), sound files (probably mp4) and videos (mp4)
    I will be collecting DNA sequences (FASTQ) and creating bioinformatics outputs (FASTA, TSV, TXT)
    I will be collecting measurements from my aquaculture experiment in a spreadsheet
    I will collect images (jpg or png, probably) and spreadsheets (CSV or excel) from a FlowCam

fieldSet.title: What is the expected size of the data that you intend to generate or re-use?
What is the origin/provenance of the data, either generated or re-used?
In which database, catalogue, or portal will you publish your raw and/or processed data
fieldSet.description:
example: 
Where publish

    I will publish my data on OBIS
    I will publish my sequences on ENA
    I will publish my spreadsheets on zenodo
    I will publish my data on my university repository (University of Somewhere, https://university.of.somewhere.eu/data) because that is mandatory, but I will also publish my data on OBIS

fieldSet.title: How will you collect the data discovery and description metadata to describe the following aspects of your data: WHO (did the work) WHEN (was the work, sampling done) WHAT (parameters, samples, data was collected) WHAT (instruments, software, platforms was used) WHERE (was the work, the sampling done) HOW (was the sampling, processing, experiment, software analysis done)
fieldSet.description:
Metadata are contextual information that you will provide when you publish your data, to make those data findable, re-usable and reproducible

EXAMPLE:
I will collect this information in a special spreadsheet: the names and orcids of all the people, the location of the sample collection, the address of the lab, the dates of sample collection and processing. I will ask the TA service provider for the names of all the instruments and devices I will use and for the protocol documents ( as a PDF or as a web address) that I will use. I will also create a document for each of my own protocols for my own processing and experimental work. I will then add all this information when I publish my data

section.title: Making data findable, including provisions for metadata

fieldSet.title: Is the database, catalogue or portal where you will publish your data Open Access ?
fieldSet.description:
by Open Access we mean that anybody should be able to find, view and download from that database/catalogue/portal

by PUBLISH we mean upload it to a public database/portal/catalogue (i.e. not peer-review) - not necessary to a scientific journal

fieldSet.title: Does the repository ensure that the data is assigned an identifier? Will the repository resolve the identifier to a digital object?
What licence will you publish your data under ? https://creativecommons.org/share-your-work/cclicenses/

If an embargo is applied to give time to publish or seek protection of the intellectual property (e.g. patents), specify why and how long this will apply, bearing in mind that research data should be made available as soon as possible.
If there are restrictions on use (e.g. IP issues, sensitive data), how will access be provided to the data, both during and after the end of the project?
fieldSet.description:
example answer: 

 I am collecting data about sensitive species, and so will keep some information (specific locations for example) private
WHAT was our decision about embargo for scientific publication? We should give an example of an answer that is about restrictions for embargo periods. Something like: I will make a metadata record for my data in Zendo, but I will embargo the data until my scientific publication has been accepted, and I will then make the data fully accessible.
Some of my data will be extremely large. I would like advise on how to publish these data and how to make them accessible for the long-term  (OF COURSE allowing answers like this means we have to read the DMPs and get in touch with the person to help them)

fieldSet.title: Unpublishable data - is there any dataset you do not want to or can't publish (i.e. make public) and why? (Null data should be published - i.e. data that is not useful to your science and hence will not be published in scientific journal should nevertheless be made Open Access)
fieldSet.description: for example - extremely large dataset, sensitive data, potential patent pending data

fieldSet.title: Will the metadata be Open Access ?
fieldSet.description:
by Open Access we mean that anybody should be able to read and download a copy of those metadata

example answer:
I will create a project-metadata-spreadsheet to collect all the metadata to describe my dataset for when I publish that data. In this I will record the names and ORCIDs of the people involved, the lat, long and marine region of any locations, dates in YYYY-MM-DD format for all activities. I will also include the name and link (URL) of all protocols that I will use (whether they are my own or from the TA station), and of all software I will use. I will ask the station for the names and types of all the instruments and devices I will use and will also write that into the spreadsheet. I will include this spreadsheet as part of my dataset when I publish it.

fieldSet.title: Are your data formats open or closed?
fieldSet.description:
A closed format needs proprietary software/application to be read (i.e. Excel xlsx vs .csv file) or it is an instrument specific format  

example answer:
My data will be in open formats - jpg, png, mp4, FASTQ
The instruments I will use at the TA station produce data in proprietary formats: I will discuss with the TA station if and how I can convert these to open formats
I will convert my excel files to CSV before I publish them, so they are in an open format

fieldSet.title: Will you use controlled vocabularies in your data?
fieldSet.description:
controlled vocabularies are terms that are agreed by the scientific communities and captured in the following examples:
https://genomicsstandardsconsortium.github.io/mixs/
https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ols4/ontologies/envo
https://www.marinespecies.org/
https://www.marineregions.org/
https://vocab.nerc.ac.uk/search_nvs/
https://agrovoc.fao.org/skosmosAsfa/asfa/en/

example answer:

I will use terms from ENVO to describe the habitats relevant to my experiment. I will use NVS to describe measured parameters and their units.
I will use MiXS when uploading my data to ENA, as this is mandatory
I do not have much experience with using vocabularies, but I will make sure that I use those mentioned in the example and will ask AQUASERV for advise.

fieldSet.title: Will all software settings be Open Access?
fieldSet.description: example: I will provided my software settings in protocols.io, or in a file in GitHub, or in a text file in my dataset

fieldSet.title: Will you provide the names and web address (URL) of all the software you used in your analysis? Is any of that software closed access (e.g. commercial)?
fieldSet.description: example: your own software at GitHub repository

fieldSet.title: Will your experimental protocols be Open Access?
fieldSet.description: you can use protocols.io for example (there is AQUASERV community there)

fieldSet.title: Please now send an email to aquaservhelpdesk@ualg.pt before submitting this DMP on zenod. We will review this DMP and provide a feedback

section.description: any other information you would like to provide
section.title: Other issues

field.textValue:
My data will be in open formats - jpg, png, mp4, FASTA
Some will be in closed format.

Within the AQUAEXCEL 3.0 programme in 2024, we generated in silico data of potential GnRH peptide family precursor sequences and potential GPCRs of the GnRH receptor superfamily. Similarly, for buccalin and its receptor. Under the AQUASERVE programme in 2025, we aim to confirm these sequences by comparison to a newly annotated genome of the mussel Mytilus galloprovincialis and subsequent PCR and sequencing of the amplicons.
I will publish on my university repository (University of Cape Town, https://zivahub.uct.ac.za/ )

We will apply for an embargo to complete replicate measurements and finalise the more-intensive work of receptor deorphanisation. This will delay publishing our processed data, until it is fully published in peer reviewed journals.
We require a period of 1-2 years maximum.
I will collect this information and will then add all this information when I publish my data in open access journals or conference presentations.
I will make a metadata record for my data in ZivaHub, but I will embargo the data until my scientific publications have been accepted, and I will then make the data fully accessible.
From experimental (laboratory-produced) work and from mining public available databases/genomes/transcriptomes.
I will collect metadata information in a special spreadsheet to describe my dataset for when I publish the data in an open access journal.

The following will be generated:
Histological micrographs as JPEG format
Molecular sequences and translations thereof in WORD/FASTA
Summary of Testing of primers for QPCRs as EXCEL format
Phylogenetic trees and multi sequence alignments in TREE file format, PDF and/or CLUSTAL formats (RTF).
